# Week 27, 2026

Highlights selected from everything kept between June 26, 2026 and July 3, 2026. Items are selected nightly by an automated pipeline and link to their original sources. See [About](../../about.md) for how selection works.

<img class="section-banner" src="../../../assets/section-digest.svg" alt="">

## The week in brief

<div class="section-brief">
<p>A pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial in 16 primary care facilities in Kenya offers some of the first real-world evidence on large language models in low-resource clinical settings. Across 9,691 patients managed by 103 clinical officers, treatment failure within 14 days occurred in 2.2 percent of the group with LLM assistance and 2.0 percent of the control group, a difference that was not statistically significant <a href="https://www.nature.com/articles/s41591-026-04503-6">[2]</a>. The authors report that LLM assistance was safe, with no serious adverse events judged related to the intervention, but that any benefit, if present, is probably modest <a href="https://www.nature.com/articles/s41591-026-04503-6">[2]</a>. This tempers vignette-based comparisons that had suggested LLMs can match or exceed provider performance on some tasks <a href="https://www.nature.com/articles/s41591-026-04503-6">[2]</a>.</p>
<p>Several reports this coverage window converge on how models are evaluated. A stress-test study of frontier models such as GPT-5 and Gemini found prevalent brittleness: systems could guess correct answers with key inputs removed yet be confused by minor prompt changes while producing convincing but flawed reasoning traces <a href="https://www.nature.com/articles/s41591-026-04501-8">[3]</a>. An accompanying summary argues that high benchmark scores do not establish readiness for medical decision support or patient-facing use <a href="https://www.nature.com/articles/s41591-026-04500-9">[5]</a>. In authentic oncology notes, GPT-4 produced reasoning errors in 23.1 percent of interpretations, often reflecting cognitive bias patterns; GPT-5.1 reduced error rates but retained the same structured failure patterns <a href="https://www.nature.com/articles/s41746-026-02951-5">[7]</a>.</p>
<p>For educators, agent-based systems show measured progress: HemaGuide reported 82.8 percent concordance in a prospective silent trial and hallucinations in 0.3 percent of evaluated cases <a href="https://www.nature.com/articles/s41591-026-04494-4">[4]</a>, while agents such as AMIE and MIRA target multi-step clinical problems <a href="https://www.nature.com/articles/d41591-026-00034-2">[1]</a>. A scoping review documents accelerating AI use in undergraduate clinical skills curricula since 2022 <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42359442?fc=20260609215449&amp;ff=20260626113417&amp;v=2.20.0">[6]</a>, and a pilot randomized trial tested prompt-guided virtual microscopy in a histology module <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42376779?fc=20260609215449&amp;ff=20260630132631&amp;v=2.20.0">[8]</a>.</p>
<p class="section-brief-date">Written from the items highlighted below; numbered links go to the sources.</p>
</div>
